characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: Accentuate the Negative: One early episode, "Recovering Pessimist", revolved around Ray's frequent lapses into pessimism, even after he wins a national writing award. Ray declares that the reason he's like this is because he learned from the masters...cough*Frank and Marie*cough. Ambiguously Bi: He and Robert discuss whether or not it's normal to check out other guys and to wonder if they might be gay. Later, he finds himself staring at a cute delivery guy, plants a very pointed kiss on Debra, and then heads to the kitchen to cool his brow with the milk carton. Annoying Younger Sibling: How Robert sees him. Attention Whore: At least inRobert's point of view:Robert:He is and always has been the center. The center of attention, the center of affection. He always gets the center chair in the kitchen, and this anger of which you speak, from the rest of these poor souls, stems from his unwillingness to share even thetiniestportion of the spotlight that shines without end... on him.[he proceeds to sit down, and Ray pokes him in the buttocks]Robert:D'OHHH! What are you doin'? What are youDOING?!Ray:What areyoudoing?[sarcastically]Center chair. It's my kitchen. Get your own kitchen.Robert:There you go, there you go, you see, because I only have a kitchenette and helovesit! Right, "Rub-it-in" Raymond? No house for me! No wife, no kids, no lawn, noNOTHIN'! Aw, Look! They Really Do Love Each Other: Eventually, as Debra and Ray got more adversarial, they would get moments of this.

Ray declares that the reason he's like this is because he learned from the masters...cough*Frank and Marie*cough. Ambiguously Bi: He and Robert discuss whether or not it's normal to check out other guys and to wonder if they might be gay. Later, he finds himself staring at a cute delivery guy, plants a very pointed kiss on Debra, and then heads to the kitchen to cool his brow with the milk carton. Annoying Younger Sibling: How Robert sees him. Attention Whore: At least inRobert's point of view:Robert:He is and always has been the center. The center of attention, the center of affection. He always gets the center chair in the kitchen, and this anger of which you speak, from the rest of these poor souls, stems from his unwillingness to share even thetiniestportion of the spotlight that shines without end... on him.[he proceeds to sit down, and Ray pokes him in the buttocks]Robert:D'OHHH! What are you doin'? What are youDOING?!Ray:What areyoudoing?[sarcastically]Center chair. It's my kitchen. Get your own kitchen.Robert:There you go, there you go, you see, because I only have a kitchenette and helovesit! Right, "Rub-it-in" Raymond? No house for me! No wife, no kids, no lawn, noNOTHIN'! characters.name: Debra Barone
characters.description: All Periods Are PMS: An entire episode concerning this has been cemented into the memory of the collective fan base. All Women Are Prudes: A shining example. Debraveryrarely gets horny — she frequently bans sex or rejects sexual advances from the desperate, horny Ray. Raydoesadmit to being bad in bed, and Debrausedto be more open — a few episodes show her rare "open" moments. Anti-Hero: In the later seasons, she becomes psychotic, demeaning and vicious. Aw, Look! They Really Do Love Each Other: Eventually, as Debra and Ray got more adversarial, they would get moments of this. Bitch in Sheep's Clothing: She becomes one after herFlanderization. Raylampshadesthis inYoung Girl, telling Robert that there are two Debras: first, there's the nice, pleasant Debra who Robert sees. Then, when Robert leaves, she turns into the DebraRaysees every day:Darth Debra.

characters.name: Frank Barone
characters.description: Abusive Parents: Frank's idea of parenting comes perilously close to this at times. In aDeconstruction, it's later revealed that the "tough love" angle was passed down through generations of heavy-handed Barone fathers, and that, in spite of verbally belittling his sons in virtually every episode, Frank could never bring himself to physically harm Robert or Ray for that exact reason.At one point he summarizes his own father's interactions with him,after coming home from long hours working on the road, as: "He'd have a drink, punish me for whatever I did and then hit the road again."Conversely, whenever the subject of his mother comes up, Frank invariably describes her as having been a saint (Marie contests this, but that seems to be more about their mother-in-law/daughter-in-law relationship than her parenting). At one point he summarizes his own father's interactions with him,after coming home from long hours working on the road, as: "He'd have a drink, punish me for whatever I did and then hit the road again." Conversely, whenever the subject of his mother comes up, Frank invariably describes her as having been a saint (Marie contests this, but that seems to be more about their mother-in-law/daughter-in-law relationship than her parenting). Actor Allusion: If Peter Boyle looked familiar from somewhere else and you just couldn't place where, then a Halloween episode where he dresses up asFrankenstein's monstermakes it clear what other role he played. Affectionate Nickname: Marie calls him "Cicci" in their more amorous moments.

characters.name: Marie Barone
characters.description: All Women Are Prudes: Two of the show's funniest moments were in episodes where Marie, of all people, subverts this trope. In "Good Girls" we learn that teenaged Marie wasn't such a "good girl" and in another episode where we learn that Frank and Marie have more sex than Ray and Debra. Apron Matron: The one most young tropers will be most familiar with. Aw, Look! They Really Do Love Each Other: Frank and Marie were this to a tee. Berserk Button: Don't call Marie a bitch. Big Bad: While it's a far stretch to call Marie a "villain," she is often the biggest instigator and antagonist in a lot of the plots. While Frank can be clueless and insensitive, Marie is deliberatelymanipulative and controlling, and often engages in complex schemes toget what she wants.

characters.name: Robert Barone
characters.description: Affectionate Nickname: Marie would often call Robert "Robbie". Ambiguously Bi: At one point, he and Ray discuss whether or not it's normal to check out other guys and to wonder if they might be gay. He implies he's glanced at attractive fellow gymgoers, admits he's not always sure, and never considers that this might be a possibility. Angrish: Occasionally, Robert will get angry at something and mutter gibberish as he walks away. Baritone of Strength: Befitting his large, intimidating stature, Robert's voice is easily the deepest on the show, and he's also a hardened police officer of 20+ years. Basement Dweller: Was one in the early seasons. He didn't actually live in the basement, but he fit many of the trope traits.

characters.name: AmyMcDougall
characters.description: Ascended Extra: Started out as an occasional guest star, gradually became involved in some of the Robert-centric plots, and finally married Robert in the later seasons and became a Barone. Beta Couple: With Robert. They were an off-and-on couple throughout most of the series, breaking up and then getting back together repeatedly. Beware the Nice Ones: In spite of Amy's unfailing kindness, several episodes show her to be tough and defensive when seriously pushed, such as when Raymond calls her an annoying chatterbox behind her back. Notably, Marierarelywins against Amy. Break the Cutie: In the later seasons, it appears that the Barones were going to give Amy this treatment after shemarried Robert. Despite several bouts of anger and stress, "Sister-In Law" being one such example,she still manages to beThe Cutieby the end of the series. Characterization Marches On: In her first few appearances, Amy's more low key and even sarcastic than she'd become. Her more outgoing, cheerful personality developed gradually. She also initially has a marked New York accent which disappears after it's established that Amy and her family are from Pennsylvania.

characters.name: Ally, Geoffrey, and Michael Barone
characters.description: Always Identical Twins: Geoffrey and Michael. The trope isdeconstructedin "Left Back": the twins' preschool teacher tells Ray and Debra that Michael isn't doing as well as Geoffrey in class and suggests that he repeat the grade, and also that they could potentially keep Geoffrey back as well so as not to separate the boys even though he's ready for kindergarten. Though they're assured that it's perfectly normal (especially at such a young age), Ray and Debra worry about this trope and end up stuck on aMorton's Fork: they don't want to split the twins up so they can maintain their bond, but realize that advancing Michael to a more difficult grade or keeping Geoffrey in an easier one is a bad idea, too. Characterization Marches On: The closest thing Ally gets to an unique character trait is that she matures into a more independent and responsible teenager over the course of the series. ​ Cool Big Sis: Ally is shown to be this to her younger brothers in a few episodes. Morality Pet: The self-centered and brash adults rein in their flaws and whatever drama they have with one another for the kids' sake. In particular, Frank has shown to be arguably closest with Ally, often showing his softer side and willingness to do things he normally wouldn't. Satellite Character: As the show's original intro explained, unlike most other sitcoms at the time, the show is not about the kids. Ally and the twins are secondary characters with no real defined personalities or traits of their own aside from being Ray and Debra's occasionally troublesome children. Their main purpose in the series isto provide situations for the adults in their family to react to without taking a prominent position in the plot themselves.

characters.name: Judy
characters.description: Sassy Black Woman: She doesn't hold back in her comments and criticism and takes no crap fromanybody. Not even Frank and Marie.

characters.name: Peggy Ardolino
characters.description: Catchphrase: In each of her episodes after the first: "Hello, Ally's Dad."Phrase Catcher: Likewise, Ray's response: "Hello...Peggy." Phrase Catcher: Likewise, Ray's response: "Hello...Peggy." Establishing Character Moment:Peggy:Now girls, when you're out selling cookies, and someone comes along, what do you say?Frontier Girl:"Would you like to buy some cookies?"Peggy:[bluntly]No. You say, "Howmanycookies would you like to buy?" Freudian Excuse: In "Sleepover at Peggy's", she mentions having a rocky marriage with a husband who was neglectful, condescending, and verbally abusive. Eventually, Peggy got fed up with him and divorced him. She outright says that her abrasive personality developed in reaction to the experience. Hidden Depths: In addition to the revelation of her verbally-abusive marriage, Peggy is later shown to be a sci-fi buff and a fan ofStar Wars. Peter falls in love immediately.

characters.name: Henry "Hank"McDougall
characters.description: Bitch in Sheep's Clothing: An interesting contrast to the rest of the cast. While outwardly polite towards everyone, he constantly criticizes the Barones on everything from their admittedly uncouth behavior to their Catholicism. For example, in "The Bachelor Party," Frank's more annoyed by Hank's passive-aggressive comments than Peter's blatantly obnoxious behavior. Gosh Darn It to Heck!: Never swears once in the entire run of the show. Holier than Thou: Condescending preachiness is one of Hank's defining personality traits. In "Meeting the Parents", Amy says that he and Pat always act judgmental and try to make everyone feel ashamed of themselves. Meaningful Name: He's the polar opposite of Frank...and note the names: Hank vs. Frank. Not So Above It All: He likes to blow up fireworks, much to the surprise of both his family and the Barones. He also mentions getting drunk at a Christmas party, prompting Frank to comment, "Damn, Hank, I'm starting to like you!"

characters.name: Patricia "Pat"McDougall
characters.description: Beware the Nice Ones: Used to hilarious effect. Cool Old Lady: Somewhat surprisingly, but very entertaining in every instance when it occurs. Ray even calls her cool at Rob and Amy's wedding, after she says to Marie what the rest of the family has been wanting to say for years. The Cutie: Not only is she this trope, but she apparently raised Amy to also personify the trope. Holier than Thou: Pat can be self-righteous just like her husband. In "Meeting the Parents", Amy says that her parents always act judgmental and try to make everyone feel ashamed of themselves. Nice Girl: Quite unlike most of the main cast, what you see is what you get with Pat: a genuinely sweet old lady.

characters.name: PeterMcDougall
characters.description: Basement Dweller: Both literally and figuratively. Big Brother Instinct: His main reason for disliking Robert. Bitch in Sheep's Clothing: Peter made it no secret that he hates Robert.So when Robert and Amy were engaged, he made every effort into sabotaging the wedding. Obviously he doesn't succeed. The Bus Came Back: Deconstructed of all things. He came back home after a long absence but his room has already been remodeled into a guest house. This forces him to confront his issues of still living at home and becomes the first step in growing up. Cloud Cuckoo Lander: His love for comics and science fiction, his friendship with his cats, and generally loopy and eccentric behavior.

characters.name: Warren Whelan
characters.description: Alliterative Name:WarrenWhelan. Informed Attribute: The characters kept describing Warren as being a heavy drinker, but we never really saw that onscreen. Obnoxious In-Laws: Downplayed. He and Lois occasionally get on the Barones' nerves, but their relationship is relatively amicable, especially compared to the Barones andMcDougalls. OOC Is Serious Business: Turns up in one episode to have a heart-to-heart talk with Debra abouthis impending divorce from Lois. Upper-Class Twit: Not a twit in the sense of being unintelligent, but he and Lois are oblivious to how arrogant and out-of-touch they often seem to others.

characters.name: Lois Whelan
characters.description: Cloud Cuckoolander: Interested in abstract art and eccentric cultural practices; she's not the most grounded person. Odd Friendship: With Marie, of all people, with the two bonding over their mutual frustrations with their husbands. Stepford Smiler: Especially in later episodes, when it's revealed thather and Warren's marriage is dissolving.

characters.name: Cousin Gerard
characters.description: Accentuate the Negative: More than even Raymond, because he's basically him without the sports journalism success (or success of any other kind for that matter).Raymond:Why do you gotta be so negative all the time?Gerard:[nasally]Because I'm not very happy... Annoying Laugh: Has one of these which is almost just as distinct as his voice. Casanova Wannabe: He joined a cult because he wanted to pick up chicks. Church of Happyology: In one episode, he's a member of a weird self-help group to which he was able to recruit Robert, worn off from an argument between Marie and Debra. It's actually a cult who wants to recruit Raymond to achieve fame. The Friend Nobody Likes: He's clearlyverylow on the totem pole inside the Barone family, whether on regard or concern.

characters.name: Gianni
characters.description: Casanova Wannabe: He pretends to be irresistible to women, but we don't see any evidence of this. Manchild: Debra considers him this. His obsession with sports and chasing women makes a strong case.

characters.name: Joanne Glotz
characters.description: Awful Wedded Life: Arguably the worst case of the entire series. She treated Robert like garbage throughout their relationship, insulting and nagging him both in private and in front of their family and friends. The moment he tries to demand respect, she uses it as a pretext for the divorce she was already planning to get. Divorce Assets Conflict: In one episode, Marie remarks that Robert lost nearly everything to Joanne in the divorce settlement, heavily implying that she was the cause of why he had to live with his parents again. Friends with Benefits: She leaves her business card with Robert when they meet again for the first time since their divorce, implying that she's interested in getting back together. It turns out she was just looking to have sex with him again, with no desire to restart their relationship. Hate Sink: Joanne is a selfish, ungrateful, spiteful bitch who treated poor, sweet Robert like dirt when they were married, and also acted like a jerk to the rest of the Barones, too. Jerkass: Joanne was verbally abusive to Robert while they were together, and she also acted unpleasant to the rest of the Barones. For instance, she told Ray he’s a bad influence on Robert, and gave Debra a backhanded compliment about her haircut, saying her hair looks less "uch" than it did before.

characters.name: Bernie Gruenfelder
characters.description: Ugly Guy, Hot Wife: Ugly guy (homely overweight) to Linda's hot wife (thin gorgeous).

characters.name: Linda Gruenfelder
characters.description: Ugly Guy, Hot Wife: Hot wife (thin gorgeous) to Bernie's ugly guy (homely overweight).
